{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

== References == {{reflist}}

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.

For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].

Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.

In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Otheruseslist|Posthumous conferment of court ranks|Posthumous conferment including decorations and titles|Posthumous conferment in [[Go (game)]]|Go tournaments|Posthumous conferment in [[shogi]]|Shogi tournaments}} {{Cite sources|date=May 2020}} '''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks of Japan|court ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==
=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period === The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[Yōrō Code]] and [[Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a rank one step lower.
From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]].
In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).
=== Modern Zōi === The practice of zōi continued into the [[Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.
Prominent figures from the late [[Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.
A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.
[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[Sakura Sōgorō]], [[Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.
The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.
From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].
=== Post-War Developments === Following the enactment of the [[Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.

{{Unreferenced|date=May 2020}}
'''Zōi''' (贈位) refers to a system in which [[Court Ranks in Japan|court Ranks]] are posthumously conferred on individuals who achieved great merit during their lifetimes. It is also called [[Posthumous honors|tsuizō]] or tsuishi. The conferment of [[official positions]] is referred to as '''Zōkan''' (贈官, e.g., Zō[[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]]).
== Evolution of the System ==

=== Zōi Before the Early Modern Period ===
The practice of posthumous conferment began in the 2nd year of [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] (673 CE), when [[Sakamoto no Kura]] was honored with the Rank of [[Komurasaki]] after his death as a ministerial official who had served with distinction during the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]]. Subsequently, the custom of zōi expanded, honoring both key figures in the Jinshin War and prominent foreign officials such as [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]]'s [[Sahachari Myōmei]], who received the Rank of Soto-Komurasaki upon his death. The [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] and [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] stipulated that for those granted zōi due to death in battle, their descendants would inherit the same Rank, while other zōi cases resulted in inheritance of a Rank one step lower.

From then onward, zōi became a privilege primarily granted to deceased high-Ranking nobles. It was also utilized to rehabilitate the reputation of those falsely accused or to honor deceased [[:en:Kentōshi|envoys to Tang China]]. 

In the case of zōi, the prefix "Zō" is added to the Rank (e.g., Zō[[Senior Fourth Rank]]). Posthumous Rank promotion, which differs slightly from zōi, is referred to as tsuishō. Zōi was also extended to monks, with clerical Ranks being conferred posthumously. For example, on the 27th day of the 12th month of [[Enchō (era)|Enchō]] 5 (January 27, 928 CE), [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] was granted the posthumous title of [[Great Teacher|Daishi]] along with the Rank of Hōin Daiwa Daishi ([[Chishō Daishi Official Proclamation]]).

=== Modern Zōi ===
The practice of zōi continued into the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] as a means to honor the achievements of deceased individuals. During this period, it was considered a new initiative to commemorate past accomplishments{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}. Following the establishment of the [[:en:Meiji Constitution|Meiji Constitution]], zōi became an [[Imperial prerogative]], with practical matters handled by the [[Bureau of Decorations|Cabinet Bureau of Decorations]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=88-89}}.

Prominent figures from the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], including supporters of [[sonnō jōi]] and contributors to the [[Meiji Restoration]], as well as [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Southern Court]] nobles and [[:en:Samurai|warriors]], were frequent recipients of zōi. For instance, [[:en:Mōri Motonari|Mōri Motonari]], the progenitor of the [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū Domain]], was awarded [[Senior First Rank]] for his contributions to the [[:en:Emperor Ōgimachi|Emperor Ōgimachi]]'s enthronement and his descendants' devotion to the Imperial court<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299200|Regarding Zōi of Mōri Motonari}}</ref>. Zōi recipients from the Edo period included [[:en:Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Tokugawa Mitsukuni]], [[:en:Matsudaira Sadanobu|Matsudaira Sadanobu]], [[:en:Uesugi Harunori|Uesugi Harunori]], and [[Mōri Shigetaka]]<ref>{{AJRC|A10110299500|Regarding Zōi of Uesugi Harunori and Others}}</ref>. Commoners such as [[:en:Fujiwara Seika|Fujiwara Seika]] and [[Mae no Ryōzō]] were also recognized<ref>{{AJRC|A10110508100|Regarding Special Zōi of Fujiwara Seika and Others}}</ref>.

A notable case occurred in 1911, when [[Naitō Hidetane]], a former cavalry officer, was posthumously awarded [[Junior Fifth Rank]] but was later found to be alive. The zōi was revoked, and the same Rank was conferred on him officially{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=90}}.

[[Campaign movements]] for zōi were often spearheaded by local communities or associated organizations, such as those advocating for [[:en:Satō Nobuhiro|Satō Nobuhiro]] and [[:en:Rai San'yō|Rai San'yō]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=97-98}}. In contrast, figures like [[:en:Sakura Sōgorō|Sakura Sōgorō]], [[:en:Chikamatsu Monzaemon|Chikamatsu Monzaemon]], and [[:en:Ōishi Kuranosuke|Ōishi Kuranosuke]] did not receive zōi despite similar efforts{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=99}}.

The posthumous conferment process involves delivering a [[certificate of Rank]] and an official decree to the recipient's gravesite or shrine, read aloud by an envoy. However, delays in identifying descendants sometimes caused postponements, as in the case of [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]]{{sfn|Oikawa Shōhei|2010|p=107}}.

From 1895 onwards, zōi for fallen soldiers was systematically regulated, granting Ranks of [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher regardless of their living Ranks, with subsequent adjustments codified in the [[Order of Court Ranks and Decorations]].

=== Post-War Developments ===
Following the enactment of the [[:en:Japanese Constitution|Japanese Constitution]], zōi became a function of the Emperor's ceremonial duties, advised and approved by the [[:en:Cabinet of Japan|Cabinet of Japan]]<ref>{{Cite web|title=Outline of Japan’s Honorary System VI.|url=https://www8.cao.go.jp/shokun/seidokaikaku/kondankai/ronten-seiri/6.html|accessdate=2023-11-29}}</ref>. [[Prime Minister’s Office]] staff managed related administrative tasks. The system continues to operate with a focus on public officials and figures of high social standing.
